data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET compatibility issue related to DSA (Digital Signature Algorithm) and the transition from .NET 10 to .NET 11, specifically on macOS. Let me think about what I know about this topic.

This is a well-known breaking change in .NET. Let me recall the details:

In .NET 7+, Microsoft has been progressively removing support for older/less-secure cryptographic algorithms on certain platforms. Specifically:

**The key issue**: Starting with .NET 7 (and continuing in later versions), `DSA.Create()` throws a `PlatformNotSupportedException` on macOS. This is because macOS's native cryptographic libraries (Security.framework / CryptoKit) don't support DSA, and .NET on macOS relied on OpenSSL for DSA support historically. Microsoft decided to drop DSA support on macOS.
 
The DSA deprecation pattern started in .NET 7 with the `SYSLIB0030` obsolescence warning, and the actual breaking change on macOS—where `DSA.Create()` throws an exception—reflects Apple's frameworks not supporting the algorithm. Since .NET 10 and 11 are future versions following this trajectory, they'd continue this pattern of removing DSA support entirely on macOS. DSA on macOS/iOS/tvOS throws `PlatformNotSupportedException` because Apple's Security framework doesn't support DSA. The user mentioned it works on .NET 10 but breaks on .NET 11, so I'm reconsidering what actually changed—likely .NET moved away from using OpenSSL as a fallback for DSA on macOS, causing `DSA.Create()` to fail where it previously succeeded. I'm realizing the core issue is that DSA was already being deprecated with the `SYSLIB0030` warning, and on macOS specifically, Apple's Security.framework never supported it—so when .NET 11 removed the OpenSSL fallback that earlier versions relied on, DSA became unavailable on that platform. The practical solutions are to either use a managed implementation like BouncyCastle, explicitly use OpenSSL interop, or migrate away from DSA entirely since it's considered obsolete anyway.

Now I'm thinking about whether to look this up or answer from knowledge, since the user is asking about a specific .NET breaking change. Given the timeline—.NET 10 releasing in November 2025 and .NET 11 in November 2026—the user is likely on a .NET 11 preview, so I should verify the actual Microsoft documentation to give them accurate information.

## SYSLIB0030: HMACSHA1 always uses the algorithm implementation provided by the platform

## In this article

The [HMACSHA1(Byte\[\], Boolean)](/en-us/dotnet/api/system.security.cryptography.hmacsha1.-ctor#system-security-cryptography-hmacsha1-ctor\(system-byte\(\)-system-boolean\)) constructor is marked as obsolete, starting in .NET 6. Using this API in code generates warning `SYSLIB0030` at compile time.

## Workarounds

Use a constructor without the `useManagedSha1` parameter.

## Suppress a warning

If you must use the obsolete APIs, you can suppress the warning in code or in your project file.

To suppress only a single violation, add preprocessor directives to your source file to disable and then re-enable the warning.

```
// Disable the warning.
#pragma warning disable SYSLIB0030

// Code that uses obsolete API.
// ...

// Re-enable the warning.
#pragma warning restore SYSLIB0030
```

To suppress all the `SYSLIB0030` warnings in your project, add a `<NoWarn>` property to your project file.

```
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
   ...
   <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);SYSLIB0030</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

For more information, see [Suppress warnings](obsoletions-overview#suppress-warnings).

The [HMACSHA1(Byte\[\], Boolean)](/en-us/dotnet/api/system.security.cryptography.hmacsha1.-ctor#system-security-cryptography-hmacsha1-ctor\(system-byte\(\)-system-boolean\)) constructor is marked as obsolete, starting in .NET 6. Using this API in code generates warning `SYSLIB0030` at compile time.

## Workarounds

Use a constructor without the `useManagedSha1` parameter.

## Suppress a warning

If you must use the obsolete APIs, you can suppress the warning in code or in your project file.

To suppress only a single violation, add preprocessor directives to your source file to disable and then re-enable the warning.

```
// Disable the warning.
#pragma warning disable SYSLIB0030

// Code that uses obsolete API.
// ...

// Re-enable the warning.
#pragma warning restore SYSLIB0030
```

To suppress all the `SYSLIB0030` warnings in your project, add a `<NoWarn>` property to your project file.

```
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
   ...
   <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);SYSLIB0030</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

For more information, see [Suppress warnings](obsoletions-overview#suppress-warnings).

Ctor\_Exception theory into two separate Fact tests: one for the null

[![@agocke](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/515774?s=40&v=4)](/agocke)[](/apps/github-copilot-cli)

ActiveIssueAttribute now extends BeforeAfterTestAttribute instead of
plain Attribute. This allows it to intercept test execution and skip
tests at runtime when conditions match (e.g., IsNativeAot).

Previously, tests with \[Fact\] + \[ActiveIssue(..., IsNativeAot)\] were
discovered by the AOT source generator (via \[Fact\]) but not skipped
(since \[ActiveIssue\] was a no-op stub), causing failures.

The condition evaluation handles:
- Type + member name conditions (via reflection)
- TestPlatforms flags (via OperatingSystem.IsXxx())
- TargetFrameworkMonikers and TestRuntimes flags

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

xunit v3 changes test execution order, which changes the shared
static Random state. This causes (-100 \* s\_random.NextDouble()) to
produce -0.0 (when NextDouble() returns 0.0). The test then compares
(-0.0).ToString() ("-0") with BigInteger(0).ToString() ("0"),
which fails since BigInteger has no negative zero concept.

Skip the ToString comparison when the expected value is negative zero.

[![@agocke](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/515774?s=40&v=4)](/agocke)[](/apps/github-copilot-cli)

The xUnit v3 AOT source generator always instantiates BeforeAfterTestAttribute
subclasses via parameterless constructors (new T()), discarding all constructor
arguments. This caused ActiveIssueAttribute to unconditionally skip ALL decorated
tests with empty 'Active issue:' messages, regardless of the specified platform/
runtime conditions.

Fix: Before() now reads the original attribute metadata from the test method,
class, and assembly using CustomAttributeData and evaluates the skip conditions
from the actual constructor arguments.
